column4: Tighten a .jd body to a minimal, useful brief. Use when a .jd reads wordy, restates its frontmatter, or explains the runner instead of the tool.
Open an agent review of the current PR. Use when the user asks to review, critique, or sanity-check a pull request.
Summarize a file or diff for the user. Use when the user asks "what does this do", "summarize", or "explain this file".
Compress or decompress a single file with gzip, zstd, or xz — the stream compressors behind .gz / .zst / .xz. Use to shrink one file (or feed a tar stream), not to bundle many files.
Archiving vs compression, and which tool reads which extension — .tar.gz/.tgz, .tar.zst, .tar.xz, .zip. Pull when choosing a format or deciding between tar, zip, and the stream compressors.
Bundle files into a tar archive and read them back — create (optionally gzip), list contents, extract to a directory, append more files. Use for the unix .tar / .tar.gz / .tgz family.
Build and read .zip archives — create recursively, list contents, extract to a directory, add files to an existing zip. Use for the cross-platform .zip format that Windows and macOS open natively.

Benchmark command-line programs with hyperfine — timed runs with warmup, statistical mean/stddev, and A/B comparison of two commands. Use to measure how long a command takes, compare two implementations, or check a speedup with real numbers.

Cross-language build/package-manager translation — install deps, add a package, build, test, and run a script written for npm, cargo, pip, and go side by side. The wizard's lookup when you know the npm verb but land in a Rust, Python, or Go project.

Inspect and test TLS certificates with openssl — decode a cert file, check a live endpoint's expiry and chain, see SANs, and generate a self-signed pair. Use to debug an expired or mismatched cert, read a PEM, or make a throwaway cert.

Cross-platform clipboard from the terminal — copy stdin in and paste the clipboard out on Wayland, X11, macOS, and WSL/Windows. The wizard's "pipe to clipboard" move, written for every environment. Use to copy command output to the clipboard or paste it into a pipeline.

Schedule recurring jobs with cron — edit the crontab, list entries, the five-field time syntax, and the @-shortcuts. Use to run a command on a schedule, read or back up existing cron jobs, or decode a cron expression.

Encrypt, decrypt, sign, and verify with GnuPG — symmetric passphrase encryption, recipient-key encryption, detached signatures, and signature checks. Use to protect a file, share an encrypted secret, sign a release artifact, or verify a downloaded signature.

Work with dates and timestamps using date — format now, convert to and from Unix epoch, and compute relative times. Notes the GNU vs BSD/macOS flag split. Use to format a timestamp, get the current epoch, convert an epoch to a readable date, or compute a date offset.

Report filesystem space with df — free and used space per mount, human-readable sizes, the filesystem holding a given path, and inode exhaustion. Use to find a full disk, see what mount a path lives on, or diagnose "no space left" with inodes free.

Measure directory sizes with du — the total for a tree, the biggest immediate children, the largest entries anywhere beneath, and a single path's size. Use to find what is eating a disk after df points at the full mount.

Attach and detach filesystems with mount and umount — list what is mounted, mount a device at a path, unmount it, and bind-mount a directory elsewhere. Use to see current mounts, attach a disk or share, or free a busy mount point.

The docker CLI surface — its object model (image, container, volume, network), the run-time flags every command shares, and how name-vs-id targeting works. Pull when wiring any docker command or deciding which object a verb acts on.

Run multi-container apps with Docker Compose — bring the stack up or down, follow service logs, and list running services from a compose file. Use to start a defined stack, tear it down, or watch what a service is doing.
The container lifecycle — run, list, exec into, stop, remove, and restart containers. Use to start a container from an image, see what's running, get a shell inside one, or stop and clean one up.
The image lifecycle — build from a Dockerfile, pull, push, tag, list, and remove images. Use to build an image, fetch one from a registry, retag it, publish it, or clean images off disk.

Observe a running container — follow logs, inspect its full config as JSON, watch live resource stats, and see its in-container processes. Use to debug why a container misbehaves without changing anything.
Manage docker networks — list, create, inspect, remove, and connect a container to one. Use to wire containers together on a private network or to see and clean up existing networks.

Reclaim disk by pruning unused docker objects — the whole system, or just stopped containers, dangling images, unused volumes, or the build cache. Use to free space, but only when the removed objects are truly disposable.
Manage named volumes — list, create, inspect, and remove the persistent storage containers mount. Use to set up storage that outlives a container, see what volumes exist, or reclaim one.

Load and unload environment variables per directory with direnv — an .envrc that activates on cd into a project and reverts on the way out. Use to scope env vars, PATH additions, or secrets to one project without polluting your global shell.

Copy, move, and remove files safely — copy preserving attributes, move or rename with overwrite protection, and delete with the guardrails that matter. Use to duplicate a file or tree, rename or relocate, or remove paths without the classic rm -rf mistakes.

Create and inspect links with ln — make a symbolic link, read where one points, replace an existing link atomically, and make a hard link. Use to symlink a config into place, see a link's target, or repoint an existing symlink.

Change file permissions and ownership — set the mode with octal or symbolic chmod, change owner and group with chown, and read the current permissions. Use to make a file executable, fix a permission-denied, restrict a key file, or change who owns a path.
How the gh CLI is driven — auth, repo targeting, and scriptable JSON output. The shared surface the gh tools build on; pull when wiring any gh command or parsing its output.
Manage GitHub issues — create, list, view, comment, close, edit. Use to file an issue, triage or inspect the queue, update labels/assignees, comment, or close.
Manage GitHub pull requests end to end — create, list, view, checkout, merge, review, comment. Use to open a PR, inspect or check out one, leave a review or comment, or merge.
Manage GitHub Releases — create, list, view, upload assets, download assets. Use to publish a release for a tag, attach or fetch build artifacts, or inspect releases.
Observe and control GitHub Actions runs — list, view, watch, rerun, logs. Use to check CI status, follow a run live, read failure logs, or re-trigger a failed run.
Binary-search the history to find the commit that introduced a regression. Use to locate exactly when a bug or behavior change first appeared.

Find who last changed each line with git blame — annotate a file, blame a specific line range, ignore whitespace-only churn, and trace a line through a move. Use to find the commit and author behind a line, or when a change was introduced.
Create, switch, list, delete, rename, and track branches. Use to manage local branches or move HEAD between them.
Apply or undo individual commits — cherry-pick a commit onto HEAD, or revert one with an inverse commit. Use to port a fix between branches or back out a change.
Read the history — log, commit graph, show a commit, blame lines, and the reflog. Use to inspect what changed, when, by whom, and to find lost commits.

Combine branches with git merge — join another branch into the current one, abort a conflicted merge, mark conflicts resolved, and choose fast-forward vs a merge commit. Use to integrate a branch, recover from a bad merge, or finish resolving conflicts.
Replay commits onto a new base, edit history interactively, or autosquash fixups. Use to linearize, reorder, squash, or reword commits before sharing.
Undo and recovery — reflog, ORIG_HEAD, git restore, reset --hard/--soft, and getting back a dropped stash or a lost commit. Use when a change was lost or a command went wrong.
The ref and revision grammar — HEAD, branches, tags, ~/^ ancestry, .. and ... ranges, refspecs, and @{...} reflog syntax. The vocabulary every git tool takes as an argument.

Sync with remotes — fetch, pull, push, and manage remote URLs. The network surface of git: move commits between your repo and origin. Use to push your branch, pull updates, fetch without merging, or wire up a new remote.

Manage the staging area — stage changes, unstage them, discard working-tree edits, and diff staged vs unstaged. The index surface that sits between your edits and a commit. Use to choose what goes into the next commit, undo a stage, or throw away a change.
Shelve and restore uncommitted changes — push, pop, list, show, drop, apply a stash. Use to park work-in-progress before switching branches or pulling.
Check out multiple branches at once in separate directories backed by one repo. Use to build, review, or hotfix a branch without disturbing the current tree.

Look up concise, example-first command help with tldr — community cheat-sheet pages showing the common invocations of a command, instead of a full man page. Use to recall a command's typical usage fast, see real examples, or update the local page cache.
Thin REST helper over curl — call any method on a URL with a bearer token and JSON body, or upload a multipart form. Use to hit an authenticated JSON API or post a file as form data.
curl flag reference — methods, headers, bodies, auth, output, redirects, retries, and the scripting flags (-fsS). Pull when building or reading any curl command line.
Fetch a URL with curl — read the body, dump only the response headers, do a HEAD probe, or save the resource to disk. Use to read or download an HTTP resource without mutating the remote.

Make HTTP requests with HTTPie — a human-friendly client where JSON bodies, headers, and query params are terse key=value args and responses are colorized. Use for quick API calls when curl's flags feel heavy, or to send JSON without hand-writing it.
Send a body to a URL with curl — POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE with raw data and custom headers, or a json helper that sets the Content-Type. Use to create, replace, update, or delete a remote resource.
HTTP method semantics (safe, idempotent) and status-code families with the common codes. Pull when choosing a method or reading a response code.

Download files over HTTP/FTP with wget — fetch a URL to disk, resume a partial download, follow links to mirror a site, and retry flaky connections. Use to grab a release artifact, resume an interrupted download, or pull a whole directory.

Convert and edit images with ImageMagick — change format, resize, crop, and read dimensions from the command line. Use to convert an image's format, make a thumbnail, crop a region, or batch-process images.

How to author a .jd shard — the frontmatter retrieval contract, the four kinds, the three invocation modes, recipe conventions, and the lint gate. The meta-shard for writing more shards. Use when adding a tool to the library, choosing a kind, or fixing a lint error.

Drive the justdown CLI itself — search the library by need, get a file as ordered sections, list categories, trace a file's @links, lint the graph, and rebuild the index. The self-referential tool: a .jd that operates the .jd library it lives in. Use to find and pull tools before doing a task by hand.

The PSAIDO scaffold dialect used in ```psaido``` blocks — imports, schemas, functions, statements, control flow, and @-references. Read, not compiled — a model translates it into the project's real language. Use to author a scaffold shard or read one a knowledge/agent file carries.
How an order total is computed and applied. Pull when implementing checkout, cart, or pricing.
The canonical Product shape (id, name, price). Pull when implementing anything that touches a product line item.

Drive a Kubernetes cluster with kubectl — list resources, read pod logs, exec into a container, apply manifests, and switch context. Use to inspect what is running, debug a failing pod, deploy a manifest, or change which cluster you target.

Convert and edit audio/video with ffmpeg — change container or codec, trim a clip without re-encoding, extract the audio track, and scale a video. Use to convert a media file, cut a section, pull the audio out, or resize a video.

Query DNS with dig — resolve a name, get just the answer, look up a record type, reverse an IP, or trace delegation from the root. Use to debug DNS, check a record, or see what an authoritative server returns.

Inspect network interfaces with ip — addresses, links, the routing table, and the default gateway. The modern ifconfig/route replacement. Use to find your IP, see which interface is up, read the routing table, or find the default gateway.

Test reachability and trace the path to a host — ping a fixed count, trace the hops with traceroute, or watch a live per-hop quality report with mtr. Use to check if a host is up, measure latency, or find where on the path packets are lost.

Inspect network sockets with ss — listening ports, established connections, owning process, and per-port lookups. The modern netstat replacement. Use to see what is listening, who is connected, or which process holds a port.

Cross-package-manager translation — install, remove, search, update, and "what owns this file" written for apt, dnf, pacman, apk, and brew side by side. The wizard's lookup when you know the operation but land on an unfamiliar distro.

Find and signal processes by name — pgrep to list matching PIDs, pkill to signal them by name, and kill to signal a specific PID. The act half of process management after ps/top identify the target. Use to stop a process by name, find its PID, or escalate a stuck one.

List open files and sockets with lsof — what holds a file or mountpoint, which process owns a TCP/UDP port, and every file a PID has open. Use to free a busy mount, find what listens on a port, or trace a process's resources.

Control process scheduling priority — launch a command at low CPU priority, change the priority of a running process, and lower its disk-I/O priority. Use to keep a heavy background job from starving the system, or to deprioritize a runaway process you found.
Inspect running processes with ps — snapshot all processes, find by name, sort by CPU or memory, and show a process tree. Use to see what is running, hunt a runaway process, or find a PID to signal.

Trace system calls and signals with strace — see what a command does at the kernel boundary, attach to a running process, filter to file or network calls, and count syscall time. Use to find which file or config a program opens, why it hangs, or where it spends kernel time.

Watch processes live with top/htop — the interactive moving view, a one-shot batch snapshot for scripts, and load/memory at a glance. Use to watch CPU in real time, capture a scriptable snapshot, or interactively hunt and kill a hog.
Session and login flow sketch — credentials in, session out. Pull when implementing auth, login, or token refresh.
Cache read-through with TTL expiry. Pull when implementing a cache, memoization, or stale-while-revalidate.
Slice a list into pages of fixed size. Pull when implementing pagination, cursor lists, or batched iteration.
The Result type — success or a typed error, never both. Pull when implementing fallible operations, error mapping, or retry boundaries.
Find files and directories by name with fd — match a name pattern, filter by extension or type, run a command per result. Use to locate paths fast, respecting .gitignore.

Find files with POSIX find — match by name, type, age, or size, and run a command per result. The portable fallback everywhere fd is not installed. Use to locate paths on a minimal system, prune directories, or batch-act on matches.

Search text with POSIX grep — match lines, recurse a tree, list matching files, count, invert, and use extended regex. The portable fallback everywhere ripgrep is not installed. Use to grep on a minimal system, filter a pipeline, or when rg is unavailable.
Query and transform JSON with jq — apply a filter, list top-level keys, extract a path, pretty-print or emit raw values. Use to slice JSON output or files on the command line.
The jq filter language — identity, field access, iteration, pipes, select/map, to_entries, length, string interpolation, and the -r/-c/--arg flags. The grammar reference behind the jq tool.

How matching works across rg and fd — Rust-regex syntax, where it differs from vim regex, fd smart-case and glob mode, and the walk flags (--hidden, --no-ignore, -g) shared by both. The matching companion to the search tools.
Search file contents with ripgrep — find matches, list matching files, count, preview a replacement, filter by type or glob. Use to grep a repo fast, respecting .gitignore.

Build and run commands from a list on stdin with xargs — one invocation for many args, one-per-line, placeholder substitution, and parallel fan-out. The glue between a finder and an action. Use to batch a command over found paths, safely handle spaces, or parallelize.

Query and edit YAML, JSON, and XML with yq — read a path, set a value in place, convert between formats, and merge files. The jq-syntax tool for config files. Use to pull a value from a YAML config, rewrite a field, or convert YAML to JSON.

Bash scripting idioms — parameter expansion, test/[[, arrays, loops, functions, redirection, and the safety preamble. The POSIX-superset every script defaults to. Use to write or debug a bash script, expand variables safely, or remember [[ vs [ rules.

Tab completion across shells — enabling the completion system, loading a tool's completion script, and generating completions from CLIs that emit them. Use to make Tab complete subcommands and flags, or wire up completion for a new tool.

Environment and PATH management across shells — exporting variables, prepending/appending PATH without duplicates, scoping a var to one command, and where each shell persists config. Use to set an env var, fix PATH, or make an export survive new sessions.

Fish — the friendly shell with sane defaults, no word-splitting, list-valued variables, and function-based config. Syntax diverges from POSIX, so translate idioms here. Use to write fish functions, set universal variables, or convert a bash snippet to fish.

fzf — the fuzzy finder that turns any list into an interactive picker. Shell key-bindings (Ctrl-R, Ctrl-T, Alt-C), the **<TAB>** completion trigger, and piping any command into a live filter. Use to fuzzy-search history, files, branches, or any stdin list.

Command history recall and expansion — incremental reverse search, history expansion (!!, !$, !*, ^old^new), and per-shell recall. The fastest way to reuse a command without retyping. Use to re-run, edit, or pluck arguments from past commands.

Job control and process management across shells — background a job, disown it, bring it back, list and kill jobs, and survive a logout with nohup or a terminal multiplexer. Use to background a long task, reclaim a stopped job, or keep a process alive after disconnect.

Nushell — the structured-data shell. Pipelines carry typed tables and records, not raw text, so you filter/sort/select with where/get/sort-by instead of awk/grep/cut. Use to query command output as data, parse files into tables, or write nu scripts.

Customize the shell prompt across bash, zsh, fish, and nu — where each shell defines it, the escape/variable for cwd, git branch, and exit status, and how to color it. Use to change your prompt, show the git branch, or add the last exit code per shell.

PowerShell — the object-pipeline shell. Cmdlets emit .NET objects, not text, so you filter with Where-Object and select with Select-Object on real properties. Cross-platform (pwsh) on Linux/macOS too. Use to query output as objects, write pwsh scripts, or translate a Verb-Noun cmdlet.

Cross-shell translation table — the same idiom (assign, export, substitute, condition, loop, list, glob) written in bash, zsh, fish, and nu side by side. The wizard's lookup when you know what you want in one shell and need it in another.

Locate a command and tell what it is — find the binary a name resolves to, test whether a command exists in a script, and reveal aliases, functions, or builtins behind a name. Use to find where a tool lives, check if it is installed, or see why a name runs something unexpected.

Zsh power features — extended globbing, glob qualifiers, parameter expansion flags, named directories, and completion. The interactive shell with the strongest pattern surface. Use to match files by attribute, recurse with **, or write zsh-only expansions.

Manage loaded SSH keys with ssh-agent and ssh-add — load a key so you type its passphrase once, list the loaded keys, and remove them. Use to stop being prompted for a key passphrase every connection, or to check which keys the agent holds.

How ~/.ssh/config and the SSH client resolve a host — Host aliases, HostName/User/Port/IdentityFile/ProxyJump, known_hosts, agent forwarding, and the -i/-p/-J/-o flags. The reference every ssh tool's host argument resolves against.
Copy files over SSH with scp — push a local path to a remote host, pull a remote path down, or copy between two remotes. Use for one-shot file transfers over an SSH connection.
Manage SSH keys — generate an ed25519 keypair, install a public key on a remote host, add a key to the agent, or list local keys. Use to set up or inspect key-based authentication.
Run things over SSH — execute one command on a remote host, open an interactive shell, or set up a port-forward tunnel. Use to drive a remote machine without leaving the local shell.
Sync directories over SSH with rsync — push a local tree to a remote, pull one down, or mirror so the destination matches the source exactly. Use for incremental, repeatable transfers.

Forward ports over SSH — a local forward to reach a remote service, a remote forward to expose a local one, a dynamic SOCKS proxy, and jump-host chaining. Use to reach a database behind a bastion, expose localhost to a server, or proxy traffic through a host.

Identify the system — kernel and architecture, the Linux distro and version, CPU count and model, and memory totals. Use to find out what OS, arch, distro, or hardware you are on, especially in a script that must branch per platform.

Read the systemd journal with journalctl — follow a service's live logs, show the last N lines, filter by time or priority, and read logs from the current boot. The companion to systemctl for diagnosing failures. Use to tail a service, find errors, or see what happened at boot.

Control systemd services with systemctl — start, stop, restart, enable on boot, and check status. The service manager on most Linux systems. Use to bring a service up or down, see why it failed, or make it persist across reboots.

Inspect and change terminal line settings with stty — read a password without echoing it, query the terminal size, save and restore the mode, and fix a terminal garbled by raw binary output. Use to take hidden input in a script, get rows/columns, or recover a broken terminal.

Control the terminal portably with tput — query size, set colors and text attributes, move or hide the cursor, and clear the screen using the terminfo database instead of hardcoded escape codes. Use to colorize script output, read the terminal width, or drive the cursor without raw ANSI.

Process text by fields with awk — run a program over stdin or a file, print chosen columns, filter rows by pattern, or sum a column. Use for column-aware extraction and quick aggregation in a pipeline.
awk programming reference — the pattern/action model, records and fields, BEGIN/END, variables, arrays, printf, and common one-liners. Pull when writing an awk program for the @text/awk tool.

Slice and reshape text with the small coreutils — cut columns by field or character, paste files side by side, and translate, delete, or squeeze characters with tr. Use for lightweight pipeline plumbing that needs no full sed/awk program.

Compare two files or directories with diff — a unified patch-style diff, a side-by-side view, a recursive directory compare, and an ignore-whitespace mode. Use to see what differs between two files, compare trees, or produce a patch.

Tidy text layout with the small coreutils — align delimited data into columns, wrap long lines to a width, and convert tabs to spaces or back. Use to turn ragged output into an aligned table, wrap prose to a column, or fix tab/space mismatches.

Stream-edit text with sed — substitute, delete, or print lines from stdin or a file, or rewrite a file in place. Use to apply a sed script in a pipeline or do a scripted find-and-replace across files.
sed scripting reference — addresses, commands, substitute flags, and the BRE-vs-ERE regex dialect. Pull when writing or reading a sed script for the @text/sed tool.

Order lines with sort — lexical or numeric, by a chosen key field, reverse, unique, and human-readable sizes. Half of the sort | uniq -c | sort -rn frequency idiom. Use to order output, sort by a column, or dedupe while ordering.

Take lines from the start or end of a file with head and tail — the first N, the last N, everything after a line, or a live follow of an appending file. Use to peek at a file's start or end, skip a header, or watch a log grow in real time.

Collapse or count adjacent duplicate lines with uniq — dedupe, prefix counts, show only repeated or only unique lines. Requires sorted input. The count half of the sort | uniq -c | sort -rn frequency idiom. Use to count occurrences or find duplicates.

Count lines, words, characters, or bytes with wc — the line count of a file or pipeline, word and byte totals, and the longest line width. Use to count how many lines match, how big a file is in lines, or how many items a command produced.
tmux's server/client model, the `-t session:window.pane` target syntax, and the shape of a `tmux <command>` invocation. Read to address the right pane from the command line.
Configuring tmux — `set-option`/`setw`, `bind-key`/`unbind`, the `.tmux.conf` file, and reloading it live. Read to change tmux behaviour or rebind keys.
The prefix key (`C-b`) and the default in-app keybinding table — windows, panes, copy-mode, sessions. Read to drive tmux interactively from inside a session.
Manage tmux panes within a window — split, select, resize, zoom, kill, layout. Use to tile a window into multiple shells and arrange them.
Drive a live program in a tmux pane — type keys, send literal text, run a shell command, and read back the pane's output. The control surface for scripting a real terminal.
Manage tmux sessions — create, attach, list, rename, kill. Use to start or tear down a named workspace the server keeps alive in the background.
Manage tmux windows within a session — create, select, rename, kill, list, move. Use to organize a session into tabbed windows.
Stage a scope and write a conventional commit. Use when the user asks to commit changes with a message.
Run database migrations and local DB tooling (migrate, rollback, seed, studio, reset). Use when the user asks to migrate, seed, or open the DB.
Build and run the project in Docker (build, up, down, logs, shell). Use when the user asks to containerize, run locally, or debug the container.
Format source files, or check formatting without writing. Use when the user asks to pretty-print or verify style.
Run the project's test/type/lint gate. Use before any release or merge to check the tree is green.
Scaffold a new .jd tool file from a template. Use when the user asks to create, add, or bootstrap a new justdown tool.
Cut and publish a release. Use when the user asks to ship a version (patch/minor/major).
Generate a report file (changelog, dep audit, file tree). Use when the user asks for a written report or exported summary.
Capture a URL to a PNG or PDF file. Use when the user asks to screenshot, capture, or render a page to an image.
Start the dev server (Vite) and keep it running. Use when the user asks to run, start, or serve the app locally.
Apply the same headless vim edit across every file matching a glob, in place. Use for a project-wide substitute, deletion, or ex script with no UI.
The vim/nvim command-line surface — the binary family and every flag that matters for opening, scripting, diffing, and remote-controlling the editor.
Vim insert-mode completion — the Ctrl-x submode (keywords, lines, files, tags, omni), popup navigation, and spelling suggestions. Built-in completion, no plugins.
Configuring vim/nvim — options with :set, key mappings, leader keys, variables, and autocommands. How to change editor behavior from a vimrc or at runtime.
Open files side by side in vim diff mode (vimdiff), including a three-way merge or a file against its last commit. Use to review or resolve differences in vim.
Edit a file in place with vim, headlessly — apply ex commands, a substitute, a deletion, or a script with no UI. Use to script vim edits non-interactively.
Ex / command-line commands — ranges, :global, :substitute, :normal, :argdo/:bufdo, sort, move/copy, read/write. The scriptable command layer vim automation is built from.
Vim folding — collapse and expand regions of a buffer by method (manual, indent, marker, syntax, expr) and the z-key commands that open, close, and navigate folds.
Vim modes — normal, insert, visual, command-line, replace, and terminal — how to enter and leave each, and the keys that only live inside them.
Vim motions — every way to move the cursor or define a range, from character steps to searches, marks, and jumps. The vocabulary operators act on.
The Neovim-specific surface — Lua config and the vim.* modules, the nvim_* API, msgpack-RPC control, and built-in LSP/treesitter/diagnostics. Where nvim goes beyond stock Vim.
Launch vim with the right invocation — at a line, in tabs or splits, read-only, restoring a session, or with no config. Use to open files in vim the way the task needs.
Vim operators and text objects — the operator + motion/text-object grammar (delete, change, yank, indent…) and dot-repeat that make editing composable.
Vim quickfix and location lists — populate from :make/:grep/:vimgrep, jump through results, and batch-edit every entry with :cdo. The result-navigation surface.
Vim registers, marks, and macros — where yanked/deleted text goes, named positions, and recording keystroke sequences to replay.
Control a running vim/nvim instance from outside — start a server, send keystrokes, evaluate expressions, open files in it. Use to drive a live editor programmatically.
Run a vimscript or a Lua script through nvim headlessly. Use to execute editor automation that lives in a .vim or .lua file.
Vim search and regex — the pattern syntax, magic levels, anchors, classes, and replacement specials used by /search, :substitute, and :global.
Vim tag-based code navigation — jump to a definition with a ctags index, walk the tag stack, and preview tags. The classic go-to-definition surface.
Vim buffers, windows, tabs, and sessions — loading files, splitting the view, moving between viewports, and saving a layout to restore later.

Run a command when files change with entr — rebuild on save, restart a server on edit, or clear-and-rerun tests. Takes the file list on stdin (from find, fd, or git). The event-driven re-run loop. Use to auto-rebuild, auto-test, or auto-reload on file changes.

Re-run a command on a fixed interval with watch — refresh its output every N seconds, highlight what changed between frames, or stop when the output changes. Use to monitor a value live, poll a status, or eyeball a changing file or directory.
Set up a fresh clone for development end to end. Use when the user says "set this up", "onboard", or "get me running".
End-to-end ship workflow — gate, release, and announce. Use when the user says "ship it" and the tree should go out end to end.
